title: Mantra Cycles and Breath: Cakrodaya and Spanda in the Tantrāloka
subtitle: The divine pulsation, breath-mantra synchrony, and the consuming of time in Āhnika 7

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's exposition of the emergence of mantra cycles in the rhythm of the breath (cakrodaya) and the pulsation of consciousness (spanda).
summary.scope: Covers the nature of mantras and the waterwheel of the breath (verses 2-5ab), the arising of mantras in the breath (5cd-21ab), the rhythm of mantra and pulse of perception (21cd-38ab), breath-mantra ritual, cycles of mantra within the breath according to the Yoginīkaula (41-52), and kālagrāsa.

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 5 (Āhnika 7), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski

body.text: The word 'cakrodaya' literally means 'emergence, arising or dawning of the wheels'. Mantras recited in consonance with the breathing cycle (prāṇacakra), depending on their length, form daily cycles of varying sizes. As the cycle of the breath does its round, the mantra recited in consonance with it arises with each turn from the heart. The dawn of the Sun of exhalation is that of mantra also. The dawning of the cycle of the repetition of mantra (mantracakrodaya) coincides with the dawning of the cycle of the vital breath (prāṇacakrodaya). (TĀv 7/1, pp. 2-4)

All mantras consisting of seed syllables (bīja) and syllabic groups (piṇḍa) are the dynamic pulsing nature (spandanātmatā) of consciousness. Thus, a mantra is said to serve as a means to attain Supreme Consciousness because such is its nature. Mantra is the pulsation of consciousness within the flow of the breath. Mantra practice initially starts in the domain of the Individual Means (Āṇavopāya). Purifying thought, it induces penetration and absorption into the energy of Śiva consciousness. Purified and freed of thought constructs, it becomes the Great Mantra (mahāmantra), which is the reflective awareness of authentic, uncreated 'I' consciousness. (TĀv 7/2cd-3ab, pp. 4-5)

A waterwheel moves a series of machines for raising water located in front of it, and possesses the varied emergent activity of the machines it sets into operation by its single, unified exertion. Similarly, when mantras arise by the strength of a single one-pointed and continuous application of attention and intention (anusaṁdhāna), the deity of those mantras, because it is at one with them, becomes propitious by virtue of that exertion. The sole purpose of the waterwheel is to make other machines work; in the same way, by the adept's single continuous application to the arising of mantras, the deity of those mantras becomes propitious without any further exertion. (TĀv 7/3cd-5ab, pp. 5-10)

The practice relating to the cycle of the breath is meant for advanced practitioners. The yogi who is engaged in the practice of cakrodaya should slowly increase the time it takes for a single cycle of inhalation and exhalation to take place. Instead of 21,600 cycles in a day and night, there should be half that number. Along with modifying the breathing cycle, it is essential that the yogi meditate on the two extremities of the movement of the breath in the heart and the external twelve-finger space. With practice, the number of breaths in a day reduces permanently. The rhythm of mantra, the pulse of perception, and the eternity of consciousness are intimately connected. (TĀv 7/21cd-38ab, pp. 10-26)

The rhythm of sensory activity, the pulse of conjunction and separation of the senses with their objects, is essentially the pulse — spanda — of consciousness. That is its nature and root cause, intimately connected to the movement of the breath. Until another pulse of cognitive consciousness arises, a single representational ideation abides as a unified whole, conceiving the diversity of manifest reality. The pulse (spanda) of cognitive consciousness is essentially its movement from one object to another, which is thus conjoined and disjoined in the sphere of sensorial cognitive consciousness. The absence of one entails the absence of the other. (TĀv 7/29-32ab, pp. 20-22)

Time does not even touch consciousness. In reality, consciousness is not 'long' or 'short' because all temporal distinctions are based on objective perceptions, and when they cease, these distinctions are eliminated. When, by the process of consuming the vital breath (prāṇagrāsa), time has been consumed (kālagrāsa), one attains the one full all-embracing consciousness. Although essentially devoid of ideation, the one consciousness, knowing in this way and that the multiple form of the objects of knowledge, is itself ideation within the sphere of phenomenal existence. Thus, the outer commerce of daily life does not cease. (TĀv 7/29cd-31ab, pp. 20-22)

Cakrodaya, that literally means 'emergence, arising or dawning of the wheels', denotes 'the rotation of the cycles of Mantra within the movement of the breath'. As the cycle of the breath does its round, the Mantra recited in consonance with it arises with each turn from the heart. The dawn of the Sun of exhalation (prāṇodaya) is that of Mantra also (mantrodaya). Swami Lakshmanjoo explains: 'Cakrodaya involves: a) The Cakrabhid — the various cycles of Mantra, and b) Mantrabheda and Vidyābheda. For example, one cakra is the ordinary cycle of 21,600 breaths of the ordinary course of breathing during one day. You have to control that cycle by lengthening the breath, for example, by doubling it in length, so that in twenty-four hours you breathe only 10,800 times. Going on gradually extending the time of a single breathing cycle, you will finally breathe only twenty-four times in twenty-four hours. Then you are the master of the various cycles and so have become the master of time.' (TĀv 7/1, pp. 2-4; TSRP pp. 65-66)

One who rests in this way progressively in the gross and (then) subtle movement of the breath, (ultimately,) the movement of (his) breathing ceases. When (in this way) time has been consumed (kālagrāsa) (into consciousness completely), this consciousness (saṁvedana), that is one and completely full and overflowing with (the wonderful) variety of energies, shines forth. It is the difference in time (kālabheda) that divides up (and differentiates) perception (saṁvedana), not difference in the object of perception. It is like the perception of one standing on a mountain peak (who, looking down, sees a great variety of things without this breaking up the unity of his perception). By consuming time (in the manner described), it assumes a nature free of thought constructs (avikalpa). Beholding in this way the entire Path of Time in the emergence of the breath and generating there itself innumerable (cycles of) emanation and withdrawal, recognising one's own Supreme Lordship, he is liberated. (TĀv 7/29cd-31ab, pp. 20-22)

Just as by (repeated) effort to move the ears, they (eventually) do so, in the same way, by (exercising) the exertion (applied to) the movement of a cycle (of the breath and Mantra), the (contemplative) mind (dhī) is present (there) within that cycle. Therefore, (as the cyclic activity of the breath is an aspect of the cyclic activity of cognitive consciousness, which is the foundation of all spiritual discipline,) the prescribed procedure is that the repetition of Mantra, the fire sacrifice, worship and the rest (should be performed) in consonance with the breath. According to the Siddhāmata, (when) the energy of Kuṇḍalinī is in consonance with the vital breath (prāṇasamā), it is the Transmental (unmanā). (TĀv 7/38cd-40, pp. 27-28)

He sets out with the scriptural association between time and the breath, to lead it on to its link with the pulse of consciousness, from that to the Void, and then pure consciousness. The (exhaled) 'vital breath' (prāṇa) is the main one, and so (it stands for) the inhaled and (all the) other (forms of the vital breath) also. The breath is established in the universal 'pulsation (of consciousness)', that is, within the modality of consciousness that impels (and imparts life), which is its initial expansion. 'The Void' is the perceiver of the emptiness (of deep sleep). Thus, the emanation and withdrawal of all things is established in consciousness, and so the innumerable pulsations (spanda) of consciousness along the cosmic Path up to Śakti are real. (TĀv 7/62cd-65ab, pp. 42-44)

Time is based on the vital breath, and the body is penetrated (and pervaded) by that. Accordingly, the nature of that (vital breath), which is established in the body, will be described. The inner (final resultant) gross state of consciousness, its pulsation and movement of the vital breath, is (called) the Cavity (suṣir). Assuming the form of the channels (of the vital breath) (nāḍī), it holds this body together (as a united whole). Thus it is said in the venerable Svacchanda that the body is pervaded by primary and secondary channels (nāḍī), like a leaf by its filaments. (TĀv 7/65cd-68ab, pp. 45-48)

gaps: The Yoginīkaula section (verses 41-52) still needs extraction
More detail on the specific stages of breath extension and the effortless practice (ayatnaja) would strengthen the practical section

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 5, Āhnika 7. Covers cakrodaya, spanda, breath-mantra synchrony, and kālagrāsa.

Expanded with 5 additional passages: waterwheel analogy detail, kālagrāsa detailed process, mantra-ritual-breath consonance, power of time/breath/pulse/void, and channels of the breath and body measure.
